title: Redpanda Demo A — Leader Pinning + Cross-Cloud Fallthrough

options.content:
**Demo A — Leader Pinning + Cross-Cloud Fallthrough**

This cluster's local Prometheus only scrapes the brokers running in **this** cloud. To see the full cross-cloud picture, open the same dashboard in each cloud's Grafana side-by-side, or open the Cross-Cloud Demo A dashboard on rp-gcp Grafana for a single-pane view.

**Reading the legends — pod names tell you the cloud.** All series are labeled with the broker's pod name (`{{pod}}` in legends), and the operator names broker pods `redpanda-rp-<cloud>-N` — so `redpanda-rp-aws-0` and `redpanda-rp-aws-1` are the AWS brokers, `redpanda-rp-gcp-0` / `redpanda-rp-gcp-1` are GCP, `redpanda-rp-azure-0` is Azure. (Each cloud's Prometheus only sees its own brokers, so e.g. on rp-gcp Grafana you'll only see `redpanda-rp-gcp-*` legends.)

**Why these panels show 'leader activity', not 'leader count':** Redpanda's public_metrics doesn't expose a per-broker leader-count gauge. The visible leader signal is **produce traffic** — only the partition leader receives produce requests, so a broker's produce rate is a direct proxy for how many leaders it is currently serving live traffic for. The `redpanda_kafka_max_offset` metric is per-replica (every broker holding a copy emits it), so a count() over it just yields replicas-held-per-broker — not Demo A's signal.

Expected pattern during Demo A:
- **T0 (steady state)**: AWS brokers carry all produce traffic for `load-test` (`ordered_racks:aws,gcp,azure` pins leaders to AWS rack). On rp-aws Grafana: `redpanda-rp-aws-0/1` produce rate ≈ full OMB load; on rp-gcp / rp-azure Grafanas: produce rate = 0.
- **Cordon + delete AWS broker pods**: rp-aws Grafana goes flat (targets DOWN). rp-gcp Grafana shows `redpanda-rp-gcp-0` and `redpanda-rp-gcp-1` produce rate climb within ~5–10s. Throughput continues uninterrupted.
- **Restore AWS**: AWS broker pods return. After leader-rebalancer settles, produce traffic flows back to AWS rack on rp-aws Grafana; rp-gcp returns to 0.

panels.title: Produce request rate per broker (this cloud) — leader-activity proxy
panels.description: sum by (pod) (rate(redpanda_kafka_request_latency_seconds_count{redpanda_request="produce"}[1m])). Only the partition leader receives produce requests, so this is a direct proxy for how many leaders this broker is currently serving live traffic for. Redpanda's public_metrics doesn't expose a per-broker leader-count gauge — this is the closest non-derived signal. Cordon-then-restore shows up as a step transfer of produce ops/s between pods.

panels.title: Bytes-in for 'load-test' per broker (this cloud) — leader-activity proxy
panels.description: sum by (pod) (rate(redpanda_kafka_request_bytes_total{redpanda_request="produce",redpanda_topic="load-test"}[1m])). Bytes the broker serves as leader for OMB's load-test partitions. When AWS is cordoned, AWS pods drop to 0 and GCP/Azure pods absorb the traffic.

panels.title: Kafka throughput per broker (bytes/sec)
panels.description: rate(redpanda_kafka_request_bytes_total[1m]). Should stay roughly constant across the demo even as leaders shift — clients reconnect to new leaders within seconds.

panels.title: Leadership transfer rate per broker
panels.description: sum by (pod) (rate(redpanda_raft_leadership_changes_total[1m])). Spikes when the leader-balancer moves leaders — expect bursts at cordon and at restore.

panels.title: Under-replicated partitions (cluster-wide)
panels.description: redpanda_kafka_under_replicated_replicas — should briefly rise during failover then return to 0 once the surviving brokers catch followers up.

panels.title: Total partitions (cluster-wide)
panels.description: redpanda_cluster_partitions — the global partition count. Reported identically by every broker, so safe to display from any cloud's Prometheus.

panels.title: Brokers this cloud is currently leading for (proxy)
panels.description: count(rate(redpanda_kafka_request_latency_seconds_count{redpanda_request="produce"}[1m]) > 0). Pods this cloud's Prometheus is scraping that are currently serving produce traffic — i.e. holding at least one leader for a topic with active producers.
